Build Your Own Digital Twin

Hardware-in-the-loop, from first principles
Case study: delta-hil
Course progress0%
    Grounded in the public repo
    github.com/harshalaptraise/delta-hil
    Module 0 · Welcome
    Module 0 · Welcome

    What you'll build, and why it's worth building

    This course teaches you to build a hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) digital twin: a real industrial controller, running its real program, driving a robot cell that exists only in simulation. You'll learn the method through a completed, public project — then adapt every step to your robot.

    The finished cell: two ABB IRB 360 deltas pick food items off a moving belt and place them into moving totes — every command issued live by a real Beckhoff TwinCAT PLC. Top-right: the TCP-vx-vs-belt overlay — bold when velocity-locked, a dot at each grab. (Embedded from assets/twincat_cell.mp4 in the case-study repo.)

    The case study

    Everything in this course is grounded in delta-hil, a project built in a compressed sprint by one engineer directing an AI pair-programmer. Nothing here is hypothetical: every code snippet is from the repo, every war story cites the actual git commit where it happened, and every number was measured on the rig.

    98
    git commits, first plant seam to published repo
    50
    tests green across 3 physics backends
    2.0 ms
    measured PLC↔sim round-trip (σ ≈ 0.25 ms)
    0
    lines of controller code changed when swapping plant or renderer
    The one idea worth stealing

    The plant and the renderer are swappable behind one unchanged controller. The same control program drives NVIDIA Isaac Sim, a GPU-free browser viewer, MuJoCo contact physics, and Rapier (WASM) physics — and git diff on the controller stays empty. Get that seam right, and everything else in this course follows from it.

    Who this is for

    Beginners: you need Python, a laptop, and patience. No GPU, no PLC, and no robot are required until the final modules — the entire method is designed so the expensive hardware arrives last.

    Senior engineers: the interesting parts for you are the sampled-data time base (Module 5), the seam architecture (Module 7), and the evidence discipline — every claim in the case study is backed by a numbered eval or an empty diff.

    What "your robot" means here

    The case study uses an ABB IRB 360 delta — deliberately one of the harder choices, because its parallelogram arms form closed kinematic chains that most simulators dislike. If your robot is a 6-axis arm (UR, KUKA, FANUC, Doosan…), a SCARA, or a gantry, several steps get easier, and each module's green "Adapt to your robot" card tells you exactly what changes.

    The route

    ModulesWhere you workWhat exists at the end
    1–2 · Rules & fakesLaptop, pure PythonA constitution, evals, and a mock loop that runs in milliseconds
    3–4 · Robot & cellLaptop (+ optional GPU)Your robot's CAD in the sim; a plant/controller/link split that never blurs
    5–6 · Clock & PLCLaptop + PLCThe real controller closing the loop, timed off its own clock
    7–9 · Seams & proofEverywhereSwappable physics/renderers, an eval table, and the road to the bench
    ⚙ From the build log

    The case-study cell was described in the project blog as built "in a day, directed by hand, one picture at a time." That compression was only possible because rules and evals were fixed before code — which is why that, not the robot, is Module 1.

    Checkpoint
    What is the single architectural idea the whole course is built around?
    Right — controller invariance is the point. Physics fidelity and rendering are swappable details behind the seam; the controller and its program never change.
    Module 1 · Foundations

    Rules before code: write your constitution

    Before a single line of robot code, the case study fixed a short list of principles that could never be broken — then derived pass/fail evals from them. This is the highest-leverage hour of the whole project.

    Why a constitution?

    A digital twin can look right while being wrong. A grab can succeed because the sim teleported the part. A latency figure can be flattering because the sim paused while the controller thought. The only defense is to write down, in advance, the handful of truths that define "correct" — precisely enough that neither you nor your AI assistant (if you use one) can drift.

    In the case study these live as executable data in src/deltahil/constitution.py, and every module in the codebase cites them by number:

    #Principle (verbatim from the repo)
    P1Real-time closed loop — the PLC free-runs on its own oscillator (un-pausable); any latency the sim adds to the loop is physically indistinguishable from real lag.
    P2I/O contract is the only channel — the PLC acts solely on its sampled tag map; unsensed plant state changes outcomes but never control.
    P3A pick is a physical coincidence — it succeeds iff pose < tolerance AND grip lands in the timing window AND force/friction suffices, jointly.
    P4Parallel closed-chain kinematics — the delta's loop closure must be honored (an articulation tree alone won't do it).
    P5Calibration corrects bias, not variance — it drives systematic error to zero but can't touch noise; residual noise floors reliability.
    P6Reproducibility is bounded by the live loop — no bit-exact replay with a free-running PLC; evals must be statistical.
    P7HIL value is conditional — worth it only if the program under test is the real deployed one; real faults, $0 core plant.
    ATwo-tier I/O — FAST tier (EtherCAT/EtherNet-IP) under the jitter bound; SLOW tier (OPC UA) supervisory, exempt.
    ◆ Design note

    The constitution file carries an explicit escape hatch: "If the build exposes a principle we missed or got wrong, STOP and re-confirm rather than pressing on." Principles are fixed by sign-off, not by momentum. That single sentence prevented several "it mostly works, keep going" moments.

    From principles to evals

    A principle you can't test is a poster. Each principle spawned at least one numbered eval with a hard threshold — written before the systems they judge. The repo even encodes which eval answers to which principle:

      src/deltahil/constitution.py
    # Which evals each module is accountable to.
EVAL_PROVENANCE = {
    "eval1_ik_error":     ("P3", "P4", "rig: 0.5 mm IK error on rigged Delta"),
    "eval5_latency":      ("P1", "A",  "rig: <10 ms round-trip, sigma<1 ms jitter, FAST tier"),
    "eval10_calibration": ("P5", "P3", "desk: bias<0.5 mm AND success<=noise ceiling"),
}

    Notice the shape of each entry: a measurable quantity, a threshold, and the principle it defends. When the rig later measured a 2.0 ms round-trip with σ ≈ 0.25 ms, that wasn't just a nice number — it was eval-5 passing, which meant P1 held.

    ⚙ From the build log

    The project blog puts it bluntly: "The hard part of working with an AI is not getting it to write code. It is saying what 'correct' means, exactly enough that neither of you can drift. Draw the ruler before you start." Later, when belt speeds changed and grip windows widened, the same evals re-ran every time. The ruler never moved.

    Write yours

    Aim for five to eight principles, each one sentence, each falsifiable. A good starter template for any pick-and-place twin:

    1. Who decides: the controller decides; the plant only senses and moves. (→ a test that the plant contains zero control logic)
    2. What "success" is physically: define a pick/weld/insertion as a coincidence of measurable conditions, never as a scripted event.
    3. What time is: the controller free-runs; the sim may never pause it. (→ a latency + jitter eval with numbers)
    4. What is conserved: parts, energy, material — pick your ledger and count it every step.
    5. What your robot's kinematics demand: the case study's P4 is delta-specific; write the equivalent constraint for your mechanism.
    ✎ Adapt to your robot

    P1, P2, P3, P5, P6, P7 and A transfer to any robot unchanged. Only P4 is machine-specific: for a 6-axis serial arm, replace it with something like "joint limits and self-collision are honored; the TCP pose comes from the real DH/URDF chain, never from teleporting a frame." For a gantry: "axes are independent and acceleration-limited." One principle should always name the thing your simulator is most likely to fake.

    Checkpoint
    Per principle P3, when does a pick succeed?
    A pick is a physical coincidence of several conditions holding at once — never a scripted attachment. The cell later added velocity-match as a third conjunct for conveyor tracking.
    Why must evals be statistical rather than bit-exact (P6)?
    The controller runs on its own oscillator (P1). Packet timing varies run to run, so correctness must be judged over distributions — thresholds on means and sigmas — not single traces.
    Module 2 · Method

    Fake it first: mocks as the golden reference

    Never build the hard thing first. Build a fake, prove the behavior on a laptop in milliseconds, and only then swap in the real component — one layer at a time.

    The two fakes

    The case study started with two mocks facing each other across the same interface the real system would use:

    • plc/mock_plc.py — a fake controller in pure Python that behaves exactly like the real PLC program will.
    • plant/mock_plant.py — a fake plant with no graphics: it streams parts, moves where it's told, and keeps the ledger.

    With both fakes, the whole HIL loop runs headless with python -m deltahil.run — including the eval-10 calibration self-score. Iteration time: milliseconds. Cost of a mistake: seconds. This loop became the permanent regression net; it still runs in CI today.

    The mock is a promise, not a shortcut

    The crucial move: the fake controller was promoted to golden reference. When the cell arrived, MockCellController defined the behavior, and the real TwinCAT Structured Text program was written to mirror it line for line. The repo says this in the controller's own docstring:

      src/deltahil/plc/cell_controller.py
    """MockCellController -- the reference cell controller (the PLC's Python twin).
…it does ALL the control -- claim a part, track it…, grip on coincidence,
transfer, track a box, place -- and clamps every command into the measured
reach envelope. The TwinCAT ST program in docs/twincat_cell_program.md
mirrors this."""
    ⚙ From the build log

    From the project blog: "When the real controller finally drove the cell and looked identical to the fake, that was not luck. The fake had already said what 'correct' looked like. The one time they differed, finding the reason took minutes instead of hours." That is the entire ROI of the golden-reference discipline in two sentences.

    Why this ordering wins

    1. Behavior is settled where iteration is cheap. Tuning a claim window takes one second against the mock, minutes against a live PLC, and hours against hardware.
    2. Divergence becomes diagnosis. With a golden reference, "the real controller behaves differently" is a bug report with a known-good answer attached.
    3. The regression net exists from day one. Of the case study's 50 tests, the large majority run against mocks on any laptop — no GPU, no PLC, no Windows.

    The interface seam

    Both fakes plug into interfaces.py — the same abstract contract the real TwinCAT link and the real physics plants implement later. This is what makes "swap the fake for the real one" a one-flag change rather than a rewrite:

    SeamFake (day one, laptop)Real (later, rig)
    Controllermock_plc.py, cell_controller.pyTwinCAT over ADS (twincat_plc.py, cell_link.py)
    Plantmock_plant.py, cell_plant.pyIsaac / MuJoCo / Rapier physics
    Rendernone (headless)Browser viewer / Isaac Sim RTX
    ✎ Adapt to your robot

    Whatever your controller will be — TwinCAT, Siemens, Codesys, ROS 2 node, a motion controller — write its Python twin first, against a plant that is just arrays and arithmetic. Match the eventual I/O exactly: if the real link will exchange millimeters as LREALs, make the mock do that too. Every unit conversion you defer is a future bench bug.

    Checkpoint
    What made the mock controller more than a throwaway prototype?
    The mock defines "correct." The TwinCAT program mirrors it line for line, so any behavioral difference is a diagnosable defect with a known-good answer sitting right there.
    Module 3 · Assets

    Get your robot into the sim

    CAD in, articulated robot out. This is the most robot-specific module — and the one where the case study's choice of a delta robot made everything harder, which makes it a great worked example.

    Step 1 — From vendor CAD to a sim asset

    Robot vendors publish CAD as STEP files (the case study used ABB's IRB360_6-1600-STD-4D STEP export). Simulators want their own formats — USD for Isaac, MJCF for MuJoCo, URDF for most of ROS. Expect the conversion to fight back. From the actual commit history:

    • 8f06d9c — the standard asset converter didn't do STEP at all; the working path was a HOOPS CAD converter.
    • 784813e — one-line API landmine: file_format_args had to be a dict, not a list.
    • b89ce15 → baa5c62 — converted parts arrived as a loose bag; assembling them into one asset needed a part-structure dumper just to diagnose why the assembly collapsed.

    The lesson is not "use HOOPS." The lesson is: budget a full day for CAD import, write tiny inspector scripts as you go (the repo keeps check_cad.py, dump_part.py, inspect_usd.py), and verify with a bounding-box check plus a render — ddda5f3 did exactly that before trusting the asset.

    Step 2 — Articulation: making it move like the real machine

    A converted CAD file is a statue. Articulation means attaching the moving parts to joints that mirror the real kinematics. For the delta this took a deliberate ladder of commits, each one visible in a render:

    1. fd6fb36 — rotate the three upper arms about their motor pivots. Just that.
    2. a73844b — full articulation: IK for the arms, forearms tracking elbow→plate, the plate moving.
    3. c3a4501 — fix the forearm roll by preserving the parallelogram's width axis (a frame-mapping subtlety).
    4. 6118e76 — articulate the 4th-axis telescoping shaft.
    5. 016a6e2 — animate one full pick-and-place cycle to a GIF. Now it's believable.
    ⚠ The closed-chain trap (P4)

    Delta robots have parallelogram forearms — closed kinematic loops. Most physics engines articulate trees, and PhysX articulations are no exception; the case study found no public IRB 360 closed-loop example to copy. The workarounds: guide joints, "exclude from articulation" flags (2a70231 made that version-robust), or — the pragmatic path actually shipped — a kinematic pose module: plant/irb360_pose.py solves the geometry analytically and poses the links each frame, with tests like test_home_pose_solves_to_home_elbow guarding it.

    Step 3 — Kinematics you can trust

    Separate from the visuals, write your robot's FK/IK as a plain, dependency-light module with round-trip tests. The case study's delta_ik.py is guarded by exactly the tests you'd hope for:

    tests/test_delta_ik.py::test_ik_then_fk_recovers_point_below_1e6_mm
tests/test_delta_ik.py::test_fk_then_ik_recovers_angles
tests/test_delta_ik.py::test_home_pose_is_symmetric
tests/test_delta_ik.py::test_out_of_workspace_raises

    That last one matters more than it looks: knowing where your robot can't reach becomes the reach envelope that Module 4's controller clamps every command into.

    ✎ Adapt to your robot

    6-axis serial arm: you're in luck — it's an open kinematic tree, so URDF/USD articulation works out of the box, and vendors (UR, KUKA, FANUC) often ship ready-made sim assets. Skip the closed-chain workarounds entirely; keep the FK/IK round-trip tests. SCARA/gantry: even simpler — 3–4 independent joints. Another parallel robot (delta, Stewart platform): follow the case study's path exactly: analytic pose module + tests, visuals posed from it, physics engine kept out of the loop closure.

    Checkpoint
    Why are delta robots awkward for most physics engines?
    Closed chains (P4) don't fit tree-structured articulations. The shipped solution was an analytic pose module with round-trip tests, keeping loop closure out of the engine.
    What should you do immediately after converting vendor CAD?
    Commits ddda5f3/baa5c62 exist because unverified assets silently collapse assemblies. Small inspector scripts pay for themselves within the hour.
    Module 4 · Architecture

    The cell: plant, controller, link — and a line that never blurs

    Three components, one rule: the plant senses and moves; it never thinks. Everything that makes the twin honest flows from keeping that line strict.

    The three components

    ComponentCase-study fileResponsibility
    Plantplant/cell_plant.pyStreams parts and totes, integrates commanded velocity (tcp += vel·dt), adjudicates whether a grasp coincided in position and velocity, keeps the conservation ledger. Zero control decisions.
    Controllerplc/cell_controller.py ↔ TwinCAT STAll the decisions: claim, track, grip, transfer, place. Clamps every command into the measured reach envelope.
    Linkplc/cell_link.pyMoves sensor and command values across the boundary. Converts units (m ↔ mm) at the seam. Nothing else.

    The separation isn't a style preference — it's enforced by tests. Two of the best test names in the repo:

    tests/test_cell_plant.py::test_no_controller_no_motion
tests/test_cell_plant.py::test_plant_holds_no_control_logic

    If nobody commands the plant, nothing moves. If someone sneaks a decision into the plant, a test goes red. This is what lets the identical plant run under a Python mock on a laptop and a live PLC on the rig without changing a line.

    The controller: one small state machine per robot

    Each robot runs a six-phase state machine. Here is the actual phase enum from the TwinCAT program — one comment line documents the whole design:

      docs/twincat_cell_program.md (Structured Text)
    phase : INT := 0;  // 0 idle  1 track  2 lift  3 transfer  4 place  5 retract
    1. idle — scan for an unclaimed part in the claim window. The upstream robot (A) claims only its alternating share; the downstream robot (B) is the greedy catch-all.
    2. track — chase the part to the window edge, then ride with it, velocity-matched, for a lock dwell before gripping.
    3. lift — clear the belt.
    4. transfer — commit to the nearest reachable tote and track it (the totes move too).
    5. place — descend into the moving tote to the live stack height, release, confirm.
    6. retract — go home, clear claims, back to idle.
    ◆ A policy with a visible cost

    One deliberate rule shapes the whole machine: only the pick chase may give up (a part can slip past before the grab — 4 s timeout). But once a part is grabbed, the robot never abandons it — carrying phases wait as long as needed for a tote. The blog owns the consequence: Robot B occasionally lets a part sail by while holding one and waiting. "I would rather have that honest limit than a demo that hides it."

    Velocity feed-forward: slave, don't chase

    The subtle heart of conveyor tracking. A naive controller commands the part's last seen position — so the TCP always lags one sample behind a moving target and the velocities never match (violating P3's velocity coincidence). The fix (commit 807b1e0): the controller commands a velocity feed-forward — the tool's X axis is slaved to the belt speed, PickMaster-style — and the plant integrates it:

      plc/cell_controller.py — inside the track phase
    if abs(x - rx) < WIN:                # in window -> descend + RIDE with it
    s["lock"] += dt                  # conveyor-tracking dwell (velocity-matched)
    s["vff"] = (vsrc, 0.0, 0.0)     # slave X to the source-belt velocity
    return (x, y, PICK_Z), s["lock"] >= self.lock_time   # grip only after the lock

    The plant then latches the grab only when |vx − belt| < 0.015 m/s — position and velocity coincide, exactly as P3 demands. The finished render's top-right overlay plots TCP-vx against the belt line, with a dot at every grab, precisely so you can see this eval passing.

    ✎ Adapt to your robot

    The state machine transfers almost verbatim to any pick-and-place cell — only the geometry constants change (reach, pick height, claim window). If your parts don't move (bin picking, machine tending), delete the velocity feed-forward and the lock dwell; P3 collapses back to pose + grip-window coincidence. If they do move, resist the temptation to chase positions. Slave the axis.

    Checkpoint
    Why does the controller command a velocity feed-forward instead of the part's position?
    A chased position is one sample stale by the time it's acted on. Velocity-slaving means the TCP moves with the belt, so the grasp's velocity-coincidence gate (P3) can genuinely close.
    Which test enforces the plant/controller boundary?
    Along with test_no_controller_no_motion, it makes the architectural rule executable: the plant senses and moves, and provably does not think.
    Module 5 · Time ⭐

    The clock is the contract

    Positions and speeds are easy. The signal that has to be right — and the one everyone skips — is time. This module is the single most transferable lesson in the course.

    The bug that taught it

    A controller runs on a hard, steady beat. A simulation only advances when you hand it a dt. Get the relationship wrong and everything downstream is a lie — convincingly rendered.

    In the case study, the first live run produced a robot that chased a part it could never catch. The sim was taking a fixed step per PLC scan, while the PLC's grip-lock timer counted real milliseconds. Two clocks, silently diverging. The trail is right there in the log:

    • c2bde5f — "advance sim in REAL time under the live PLC (fixes no-grab)" — first fix: wall-clock time.
    • 7c8cad2 — the real fix: "Derive sim dt from the PLC's own clock (sampled-data time base) + wall-clock fallback."

    The fix: one clock, the controller's

    The PLC publishes its own clock as a tag (GVL_Cell.plc_time_ns). Every loop iteration, the sim advances by exactly the time the PLC says elapsed between samples — which also self-corrects for jitter in when packets land:

      scripts/run_twincat_cell.py — the live loop (verbatim)
    # SAMPLED-DATA: advance the continuous plant by the PLC's OWN elapsed time
# between samples. Fall back to the wall clock if the PLC doesn't publish it.
if plc_ns is not None and prev_plc is not None:
    rdt = min((plc_ns - prev_plc) / 1.0e9, 0.05)   # PLC clock; clamp MAX only
    clock_src = "PLC clock (GVL_Cell.plc_time_ns)"
else:
    rdt = min(max(now - prev, 0.001), 0.05)        # wall-clock fallback

    One refinement rides along (0d22df7): the plant sub-steps between coarse PLC ticks — up to 8 fine integration steps per sample — so fast tracking stays smooth without changing the control sample rate. One command per sample, finer physics in between. That's textbook sampled-data control, discovered the hard way.

    Your latency budget, made concrete

    Loop delay is not an abstract quality metric — it is your aiming error. A part on a belt moving at v m/s, sensed t ms late, has moved v·t mm by the time the controller's command lands. The case study measured 2.0 ms round-trip, σ ≈ 0.25 ms over ADS: at a 0.22 m/s belt that's ~0.44 mm of error — comfortably inside grip tolerance. Try your own numbers:

    Interactive · Latency → aiming-error budget
    0.22 m/s
    2.0 ms
    3.0 mm

    The blog's rule of thumb: "Two milliseconds is a green light. Ten with five [ms of jitter] would be a warning to redesign the belt before ordering a motor." A real cell replaces polled ADS with a hard fieldbus that is tighter still — so if the twin's timing passes, the bench inherits the margin.

    The deeper insight: a plant that flows, a mind that ticks

    The sharpest design exchange of the build wasn't about code. The obvious way to sync sim and controller is hard lockstep under one master clock. The pushback: a real plant flows — it moves continuously, with no clock and no ticks. Forcing it into lockstep imposes digital rigidity on the one component that, in reality, has none. A control system is a flowing plant checked now and then by a ticking controller, with real delay in between. Seen that way, the humble polling loop isn't a compromise — it's the honest shape of sampled control.

    ✎ Adapt to your robot

    Whatever your controller, make it publish its own time base as a first-class tag/topic (nanoseconds since start is fine), and derive the sim's dt from it — with a wall-clock fallback so older controller programs still run. If you're on ROS 2, the same discipline applies: drive the sim from the controller node's clock, not the sim's own timer. Then run your version of eval-5 and write the measured latency and jitter into the README like a spec.

    Checkpoint
    The "no-grab" bug happened because…
    Commits c2bde5f → 7c8cad2 tell the story: the fix was to make the PLC's own published clock the single time base for the sim.
    A part moves at 0.5 m/s and your loop latency is 10 ms. How far has it moved when your command lands?
    v·t = 0.5 m/s × 0.010 s = 5 mm. If your grip tolerance is ±3 mm, that latency alone already blows the budget — redesign before ordering hardware.
    Module 6 · Hardware-in-the-loop

    Close the loop with the real controller

    This is the moment the project becomes HIL: the Python mock steps aside and a real Beckhoff TwinCAT PLC — running the mirrored ST program — takes command of the cell.

    Staged bring-up, never a big bang

    The case study never connected everything at once. The rungs, each one a shipping script:

    1. run_twincat_mock.py — real PLC ↔ mock plant, no graphics at all (pure pyads). The quickest proof the real controller closes the loop, and where eval-5 latency is first measured.
    2. run_twincat_loop.py — real PLC drives the kinematic robot in the sim.
    3. run_twincat_render.py — real PLC drives the fully articulated CAD robot → GIF.
    4. run_twincat_cell.py <AMS_NET_ID> — the full two-robot cell, live.

    Each rung isolates one new risk. When rung 4 misbehaves, rungs 1–3 tell you which layer to suspect.

    The transport: one write, one read

    Communication runs over ADS (Beckhoff's protocol; pyads on the Python side). The first implementation made one ADS call per tag — and commit c51fc1e records the fix: batch reads/writes via sum-commands, cutting round-trips ~3×. The final shape is exactly two operations per control sample:

      plc/cell_link.py — one sum-read returns commands + enable + the PLC clock
    def read_commands(self):
    """Returns (cmds, enable, plc_time_ns). plc_time_ns is the PLC's own clock
    in ns (the sim derives dt from it); None if the PLC doesn't publish it ->
    the caller falls back to the wall clock. enable=False -> freeze."""

    Note the graceful degradation built into the link: if the PLC program is an older version without the velocity or clock symbols, the link detects the missing tag, drops it, and keeps running. Version drift between PLC program and Python code is a fact of life; design the seam to survive it.

    Units live at the seam — and nowhere else

    The plant thinks in meters; the PLC thinks in millimeters (LREAL). The conversion happens exactly once, inside cell_link.py (c * 1000.0 on the way in, / 1000.0 on the way out). Pick your convention, write it into the GVL spec (docs/twincat_gvl_spec.md in the repo), and never convert anywhere else.

    Prove the PLC is really in charge

    A twin driven by a real controller should be freezable from the controller. Commit f3c4027 added GVL_Cell.enable — flip it to FALSE in a TwinCAT Watch window and the entire simulated cell freezes mid-motion. It's a one-boolean demonstration that control authority lives in the PLC, not in Python. Show this to a skeptical audience before you show them anything else.

    ⚙ War story: the paste that broke everything

    The ST program is distributed as markdown (docs/twincat_cell_program.md) to paste into TwinCAT panes. One bring-up session died in a cascade of "identifier not defined" errors. Root cause (commit be7cdcb): the FUNCTION_BLOCK header hadn't been pasted along with the body. A sibling commit (7aefbac) reformatted VAR blocks to one declaration per line for paste safety. Painfully mundane — which is exactly why it's documented: the failure mode of PLC bring-up is rarely exotic.

    What eval-5 looked like when it passed

    2.0 ms
    mean ADS round-trip, full cell (threshold: <10 ms)
    0.25 ms
    jitter σ (threshold: <1 ms)
    ~3×
    round-trips saved by sum-command batching
    ✎ Adapt to your robot / controller

    Siemens: swap ADS for OPC UA or Snap7; keep the sum-read/sum-write shape (one exchange per direction per sample). Allen-Bradley: the repo already stubs it — plc/logix_plc.py — via EtherNet/IP tag reads. ROS 2: the "link" is a pair of topics; the discipline (units at the seam, controller clock published, enable flag, graceful degradation) is identical. Whatever the transport, write the tag map down as a spec document first, like twincat_gvl_spec.md.

    Checkpoint
    Why does the link batch all tags into one sum-read and one sum-write per sample?
    Commit c51fc1e. Every extra round-trip is added loop latency, and Module 5 showed latency is aiming error. Two exchanges per sample is the floor.
    What does flipping GVL_Cell.enable to FALSE in a Watch window demonstrate?
    It's the one-boolean proof of HIL: the thing deciding when everything moves is the real factory controller, not the Python script.
    Module 7 · The payoff

    Swap the seams: four plants, two renderers, zero controller changes

    Everything so far built one seam-shaped system. Now collect the payoff: swap physics engines and renderers like lenses, while git diff on the controller stays empty.

    The architecture, clickable

    Click any block to see what it is and what swaps behind it:

    👆 Click a block in the diagram.

    What each swap proved

    BackendCommitWhat it demonstrated
    Web viewer (Three.js)663aadaThe renderer is a seam: the whole cell runs GPU-free and offline in a browser; --realbot (fe3f4d9) articulates the real ABB CAD via a JS port of the pose module.
    MuJoCo plante8e9094The plant is a seam: real contact physics — a friction-driven belt (0.220 m/s emergent, nobody scripts the motion), a weld grasp gated by P3, parts that physically pile in totes.
    Rapier planta6c9060The seam generalizes: a second, totally different engine (Rust/WASM, driven through a node worker over stdio) behind the same contract.
    The proof, not the claim

    The eval table doesn't say "the architecture is clean." It says: controller invariance — git diff empty — cell_controller, cell_link, and the ST program are byte-identical across all backends, and cross-backend agreement — kinematic, MuJoCo, and Rapier place counts match within tolerance under the same controller. Those are checkable facts, and tests like test_cross_backend_agreement_with_kinematic keep them true.

    Why bother with multiple physics engines?

    Three reasons, in ascending importance: (1) reach — a browser viewer means anyone can run your twin without an RTX workstation; (2) trust — a skeptic who distrusts kinematic grasp adjudication can watch MuJoCo's contact solver reach the same throughput; (3) the bench rehearsal — swapping the kinematic plant for MuJoCo is a laptop-scale dress rehearsal of the real swap coming in Module 8: replacing the simulated plant with steel.

    ✎ Adapt to your robot

    You don't need four backends. You need two: your fast default plant, plus one alternative behind the same contract — that second implementation is what forces the seam to be real. A GPU-free viewer is worth disproportionate effort: it's what lets students, reviewers, and managers actually see your cell.

    Checkpoint
    What is the concrete evidence that the controller is truly independent of the plant?
    Claims are cheap; an empty diff and a passing agreement test are checkable. This "proof, not claim" habit is what makes the project credible to senior engineers.
    Module 8 · Evidence

    Prove it: evals, ledgers, and debugging by screenshot

    A twin earns trust the way any instrument does — by publishing what it measures, what it claims, and where it still fails.

    The eval table as a public scoreboard

    The case-study README ends every claim at a measured row. A selection, verbatim:

    EvalStatus
    5 · <10 ms / σ<1 ms round-trip (P1, A)MET cell ADS ≈ 2.0 ms, σ ≈ 0.25 ms
    velocity-lock at pick (P3)MATCHED grab latches only at |vx−belt| < 0.015 m/s
    reach envelope never violated0 violations in mock + live
    controller invariance (every plant + renderer)git diff empty
    MuJoCo belt carries by friction (P3)0.220 m/s emergent — no one scripts the motion
    MuJoCo grasp needs velocity coincidence (P3)REJECTED pose-only, zero-velocity contact
    cross-backend agreement (kinematic ↔ MuJoCo ↔ Rapier)within tolerance same controller
    10 · calibration bias<0.5 mm, success ≤ noise ceiling (P5)self-scored PASS

    Notice the negative evals: the MuJoCo grasp test proves a pose-only, zero-velocity contact is rejected. Tests that confirm your system refuses to succeed for the wrong reason are worth more than ten happy-path tests.

    The conservation ledger

    Every part is counted, every step: picked / placed / passed / conserved. Any part that vanishes — clipped through a belt, deleted by a solver hiccup, double-grabbed — breaks the ledger instantly. It's the cheapest whole-system integrity check you can build, and it caught real geometry bugs (see f946365, 5f51ade — belt/forearm collisions and TCP penetration found because outcomes didn't add up). Calibration has the same evidence discipline: eval-10 requires bias < 0.5 mm and that success doesn't exceed the noise ceiling — because per P5, calibration corrects bias, never variance, and a result better than the ceiling means you're fooling yourself.

    Debugging by screenshot: one clear complaint

    The build's iteration loop — human runs the render, pastes a screenshot, names one checkable flaw — is visible in the commit log as a rhythm of specific complaints and specific fixes:

    • "The boxes overlapped" → cc250f2 fix box overlap
    • "Robot B stood still while a reachable part sailed past" → dfe8455 dual-robot load balancing
    • "The TCP snaps instead of chasing" → 0f1fed8 TCP chases the part's real position
    • "B misses at the root" → 060728d sub-step the plant vs. coarse PLC ticks

    From the blog: "Every time I named the flaw in one sentence, it got fixed. Every time I waved my hand and said 'make it better,' nothing did." A digital twin is a way to watch before it is anything else — the render isn't decoration, it's the ruler.

    ⚠ Publish your limitations

    The case study ships its flaw on the front page: Robot B occasionally lets a part escape while holding one, a direct consequence of the "never abandon a grabbed part" rule. It also published an honest novelty assessment — every ingredient (conveyor tools, trained grippers, the PLC-sim bridge, conveyor tracking itself) exists publicly on its own; the narrow new thing is the combination. Being precise about the pieces is what makes the combination believable. Do the same in your README.

    Checkpoint
    Why is the "pose-only, zero-velocity contact rejected" test especially valuable?
    Happy-path tests can pass by accident. A test that demands failure under almost-right conditions pins down the mechanism.
    What made the screenshot-feedback loop effective?
    "Make it better" produced nothing; "Robot B stood still while a reachable part sailed past" produced a commit. Specificity is the entire trick.
    Module 9 · Your build

    Your robot, your twin: the roadmap

    Everything above, folded into a checklist you can execute — plus what changes when the twin finally meets the bench.

    The build checklist

    Tick items as you complete them (progress is saved in this file's browser storage):

    Taking it to the bench

    The twin's whole purpose is to be the on-ramp to hardware. The accounting, from the case study:

    What
    STAYSThe entire controller program, unchanged — that is the point of HIL. The tag map. The sampled-control discipline. The sim itself, flipped from plant to shadow: running alongside the real line for comparison.
    GOESThe plant's grasp adjudication (real physics, a real gripper, and cameras decide now). The soft polled time base (a hard fieldbus with a shared clock replaces it). The Python stand-in.
    ARRIVESThe expensive half: real robots and drives, fieldbus, vision, belt encoders, safety interlocks — and calibration to make robot and camera frames agree to a fraction of a millimeter (your P5 eval, now on steel).

    You debug the mind in software, where a mistake costs seconds, so on the bench you're only debugging the body. The clock was the contract in the twin; it will be on the bench. Only the hands change.

    If you remember three things

    ① Rules before code — a constitution with numbered, falsifiable evals. ② The clock is the contract — drive the sim from the controller's own time base and treat latency as aiming error. ③ Prove seams with diffs, not diagrams — one unchanged controller across two plants beats any architecture slide.

    Go further

    • Run the case study yourself: pip install -e ".[web,dev]" → python -m pytest -q → python scripts/run_web_cell.py --realbot → open http://127.0.0.1:8080. No GPU, no PLC needed.
    • Read the code in course order: constitution.py → plc/mock_plc.py → plant/cell_plant.py → plc/cell_controller.py → plc/cell_link.py → scripts/run_twincat_cell.py.
    • The write-up: the project blog, "First Principles in Motion," covers the working method — directing an AI with a constitution and one clear complaint at a time.
    Final checkpoint
    When the twin moves to the bench, what stays exactly the same?
    That gap — controller unchanged, senses swapped — is the entire reason to build the twin. Congratulations: you've finished the course. 🎓
